coredata.dc:title: Tuning of carbon dots emission color for sensing of Fe3+ ion and bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
Ha, Siyoung
Baek, Seung Hoon
Phan, Le Minh Tu
Kim, Sangjun
Kwak, Kyungwon
Park, Tae Jung
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a facile one-step synthetic strategy for fabrication of three (blue, green and yellow) fluorescent color carbon dots (CDs) from tomato (Solanum lycopersicum). The as-synthesized CDs showed emission peaks at 450, 520 and 560 nm for blue, green and yellow color CDs when excited at 370, 420 and 460 nm, respectively. Using tomato as a carbon source, the fabricated three fluorescent color CDs showed good water dispersity and high quantum yield. The analytical performances of three fluorescent color CDs are evaluated by detecting Fe3+ ion in biofluids and iron tablets. Upon the addition of Fe3+ ion under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of three fluorescent color CDs was quenched linearly over the range of 0.1 to 2.0 μM. This method opens a new analytical strategy to quantify Fe3+ ion in iron tablets and biofluids with high sensitivity. Further, the uptake of three fluorescent color CDs into HeLa cells was confirmed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. Intracellular experiments demonstrated that the three fluorescent color CDs were effectively internalized the cells and show excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, suggesting that the CDs can be used as good candidates for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Fe3+ ion
Cancer cells
Tomato
Fluoresce spectroscopy and microscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green synthetic approach for fabrication of blue, green and yellow CDs
Spectroscopic characteristics of blue-, green-, and yellow-CDs
Evaluation of analytical performance of three fluorescent CDs
Assay of Fe3+ ion in biofluids and pharmaceutical samples
Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells using three fluorescent CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV − visible absorption, fluorescence emission excitation and excitation spectra of (a) blue- (λex = 360 nm; λem = 450 nm), (b) green- (λex = 420 nm; λem = 520 nm), and yellow- (λex = 460 nm; λem = 560 nm) CDs. Inset: photographic images of blue-, green- and yellow- CDs under visible light and UV light illumination at 365 nm. Fluorescence emission spectra of (d) blue-, € green- and (f) yellow- CDs at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 310–410, 380–480, and 390–500 nm for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs.

HR-TEM images of (a) blue-, (b) green- and (c) yellow- CDs. HR-TEM images of (d) blue-, (e) green- and (f) yellow-CDs with addition of Fe3+ ion. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) blue-, (b) green- and (c) yellow-CDs upon the addition of various metal ions Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ag+ and As5+ ions (200 μM). Fluorescence emission spectra of (d) blue-, (e) green- and (f) yellow-CDs with the addition of Fe3+ ion ranging from 0.1 to 2.0 μM. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence responses of (a) blue-, (b) green- and (c) yellow-CDs in the presence of mixture of inorganic species (metal ions - Ba2+, Ca2+,Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe2+ and Al3+ ion, and anions - Cl−, F−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, S2−, PO4 3− and Cr2O7 2−, 100 μM) without and with addition of Fe3+ ion. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells by using (a) blue-, (b) green- and (c) yellow-CDs as fluorescent probes. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Tuning of carbon dots emission color for sensing of Fe3+ ion and bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Interdisciplinary Convergence Research, Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Molecular Spectroscopy and Dynamics, Institute for Basic Science (IBS), Korea University, Seoul 02841, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Molecular Spectroscopy and Dynamics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Korea University, Seoul 02842, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a facile one-step synthetic strategy for fabrication of three (blue, green and yellow) fluorescent color carbon dots (CDs) from tomato (Solanum lycopersicum). The as-synthesized CDs showed emission peaks at 450, 520 and 560 nm for blue, green and yellow color CDs when excited at 370, 420 and 460 nm, respectively. Using tomato as a carbon source, the fabricated three fluorescent color CDs showed good water dispersity and high quantum yield. The analytical performances of three fluorescent color CDs are evaluated by detecting Fe3+ ion in biofluids and iron tablets. Upon the addition of Fe3+ ion under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of three fluorescent color CDs was quenched linearly over the range of 0.1 to 2.0 μM. This method opens a new analytical strategy to quantify Fe3+ ion in iron tablets and biofluids with high sensitivity. Further, the uptake of three fluorescent color CDs into HeLa cells was confirmed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. Intracellular experiments demonstrated that the three fluorescent color CDs were effectively internalized the cells and show excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, suggesting that the CDs can be used as good candidates for biomedical applications.

Schematic representation for the preparation of blue, green and yellow color carbon dots using Solanum lycopersicum fruits. Evaluation of their analytical applications for sending of Fe3+ ion and imaging of HeLa cells.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention in various scientific communities ever since their generations from carbon nanotubes [1,2]. The CDs are <10 nm size with discrete quasi-spherical amorphous nature, which exist as rich surface organic functional groups [3]. They have evolved as a new class of functional nanostructure materials and well-suited for various applications including sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, catalysis, biological monitoring, photovoltaic and optoelectronic devices [4–8]. Importantly, they have endowed with high productivity yields using cost-effective precursors (natural resources) as well as biocompatibility, high water dispersibility, small size, high brightness with high quantum yields (Φs), and non-toxicity, which make them as popular probes in various scientific applications [9,10]. In view of this, fluorescent CDs have been accessed by various synthetic routes including laser ablation, arc discharge, chemical oxidation, electrochemical methods, microwaves, thermal combustion, hydrothermal and solvothermal techniques, respectively [8,11,12]. Several research groups have been focused on the development of green synthetic approaches for the fabrication of fluorescent CDs using various natural resources as precursors such as potato [12], pasteurized milk [13], apple juice [14], sugar cane juice [15], Punica granatum [16], dried shrimp [17], water melon peel [18], jamun [19], corn flour [20], chicken egg [21], and orange juice [22], respectively. The synthesized CDs have been successfully applied in sensing of various analytes and bioimaging of cells. However, they have some minor defects i.e., single emission peak, and low Φs. Obviously, novel carbon dots with multi-emission peaks and colors are essentially needed for improving the performance of fluorescence technique in sensing and bioimaging applications.

Ferric ion (Fe3+ ion) is one of the essential trace elements that play dominant roles in various biochemical pathways [23]. Due to its unique redox and coordination properties, it acts as a cofactor or key structural element for many critical biological functions including oxygen transport, electron transfer, cell respiration, DNA and hemoglobin synthesis [24]. Its deficiency and excess amount in a biological system cause various disorders such as anemia, renal failure, cancer, heart disease and liner injury [25]. To regulate all Fe3+ ion biological functions, it should be within an optimal physiologic range, and Fe3+ ion concentration was accurately monitored in biological and environmental samples. Currently, various spectroscopic methods including UV–visible [26], fluorescence [27–30], atomic absorption spectrometry [31], inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometric [32] and mass spectrometric [33] techniques have been adopted for the analysis of Fe3+ ion in various samples. Apart from these, electrochemical [34], and amperometeric [35] methods were reported for the estimation of Fe3+ ion concentration in real samples. However, they require tedious sample preparation protocols, and sophisticated instrumentation. Further, expensive chemicals are required for the synthesis of organic receptors (ligands). Therefore, it is necessary to develop green fluorescent probes that highly selective toward Fe3+ ion over the other trace elements, facilitating to accurate detection of Fe3+ ion in complex samples.

Recently, CDs-based fluorescence methods have been received significant interest in identifying inorganic species including Fe3+ ion with high degree sensitivity [36,37]. For example, Fe3+ ion was successfully detected in various samples using fluorescent CDs derived from various precursors such as ethanol [37], potato [38], Jinhua bergamot [39], honey [40], Prunus avium fruit [41], garlic [42], acrylic acid-methionine [43], Lycii Fructus [44], and rose-heart radish [45], respectively. We report here analytical performance of three fluorescent CDs (blue, green and yellow) for assaying Fe3+ ions and bioimaging of cancer cells.

Herein, we explore the analytical performances of three fluorescent color CDs (blue, green and yellow) for fluorescence “turn-off” detection of Fe3+ ion in biological and pharmaceutical samples. The blue, green and yellow color fluorescent CDs were derived from tomato (Solanum lycopersicum fruit) using H2SO4 and H3PO4 as oxidizing agents (Scheme S1 in Supporting Information). The three fluorescent CDs exhibited high selectivity to bind with Fe3+ ion, resulting to quench fluorescence of CDs, which exhibit good linearity between F0/F and concentration of Fe3+ ion (0.10–1.25 μM). Compared to organic-based fluorescent probes, the present method exhibits advantages such as generation of three CDs, rapid response (30 min), low limit of detection (LOD) (0.016 μM). Additionally, we evaluated the performance of three CDs for assaying Fe3+ ions via fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism, blue-CDs exhibited lower LOD than the green- and yellow-CDs. The use of three-color CDs as imaging probes has also demonstrated by imaging of HeLa cells, suggesting that these CDs serve as the promising agents for imaging of cells with good resolution, which may expand their potential use in analytical and bioanalytical sciences.

Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum fruit) was purchased from local market, Seoul, Korea. Metal salts and acids were procured from DaeJung Chemicals (Siheung, Korea), Junsei Chemical (Tokyo, Japan), and Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Methyl thiazolyl diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000 Da) was purchased from Spectrum Laboratories (Milpitas, CA, USA). All analytical grade chemicals were used without any further purification. All the experiments were performed by using Milli-Q-purified deionized (DI) water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthetic approach for fabrication of blue, green and yellow CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tomato (100 g) was washed and then sliced. The tomato slices were kept at −20 °C in a deep freezer. The blue, green and yellow color CDs were fabricated by the modification of described procedure in the literature [46]. To fabricate blue-CDs, 0.5 g of freeze tomato slices was taken into a 100 mL beaker that contains 5 mL DI water. To this, 34 N of H2SO4 (10 mL) was added and sonicated for 5 min. The mixture of heated at 100 °C for 1.0 h. The obtained black residue was neutralized with 1.0 M of NaOH and then dialyzed against DI water using Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000 Da) for 48 h. To fabricate green-CDs, freeze dried tomato (0.5 g) was taken into a 100 mL beaker that contains 5 mL of DI water. To this, 10 mL of 40 N H3PO4 and heated at 80 °C for 25 min. The green CDs were isolated from the obtained crude product by the above-described procedure. Similarly, yellow-CDs were fabricated by treating 0.5 g of freeze dried tomato with 40 N H3PO4 (10 mL), and the mixture was heated at 80 °C for 20 min. The obtained crudes were neutralized with 1.0 M of NaOH and then dialyzed against DI water using Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000 Da) for 48 h. The obtained blue-, green- and yellow-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further experiments. Characterization, estimation of quantum yield, assay of Fe3+ ion, cytotoxicity and cell imaging procedures were described in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic characteristics of blue-, green-, and yellow-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The blue-, green-, and yellow-color fluorescent CDs were fabricated by using tomato as a carbon source. This method provides a simple green synthetic strategy to fabricate three fluorescent color CDs using H2SO4 and H3PO4 as oxidizing agents at moderative temperatures. Scheme S1 in Supporting Information represents the fabrication of three fluorescent color CDs using tomato as a source and their applications in Fe3+ ion sensing and HeLa cell imaging. The as-fabricated fluorescent CDs solutions are transparent, and the UV/vis spectra of CDs showed the maximum absorption at 260, 280 and 285 nm for blue-, green-, and yellow-CDs, respectively. Specifically, the absorbance at 260 nm corresponds to π-π* transition of sp2 carbons in aromatic compounds. The absorbances at 280 and 285 nm are attributed to n-π⁎ transition of carbonyl and CN bonds. These absorption characteristics reveals that the existence of π → π* and n → π* transitions in three CDs, which are due to the presence of organic functional groups (CO, NH2, COOH and CHO). The visual emission colors of CDs were examined under UV light irradiation at 365 nm (Inset of Fig. 1 ), confirming the formation of CDs with three specific emission colors, i.e., blue, green and yellow. Fascinatingly, as-fabricated three CDs exhibited emission peaks at 450, 520 and 560 nm when excited at wavelengths of 360, 420, 460 nm for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs, respectively (Fig. 1a–c). Similarly, the excitation-dependent emission spectra of three CDs were measured by using different excitation wavelengths from 310 to 410, 380 to 480 and 390 to 500 nm for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs (Fig. 1d–f). It can be noticed that the maximum emission peaks were observed at 450, 520 and 560 nm when excited at 360, 420, and 460 nm for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs, respectively. These results revealed that the emission wavelengths of CDs were red-shifted by increasing the concentration of phosphoric acid, confirming the phosphorus was effectively co-doped into the fabricated green- and yellow-CDs, which plays a key role in tuning the emission wavelengths of CDs [37]. Since the addition of phosphorus into nanostructures of CDs leads to generate more many number of isolated sp2 carbon clusters and defect sites, which can change the optical properties of CDs [46]. As a result, the sizes of CDs slightly increase due to the extension of an electron system, favoring to increase π-π conjugation in CDs, which causes the red-shift in the emission wavelength of CDs.

Thus, the emission peaks were red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelengths of three CDs, signifying the formation of three CDs with different size variations as well as the existence of different surface states distribution, which is due to the presence of multi-functional organic groups on the surfaces of CDs [37,46]. The quantum yields were found to be 12.70%, 4.21% and 2.76% for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs, respectively.

The FT-IR spectra were studied to investigate the surface organic groups of three carbon dots (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information). The broad band around at 3200–3550 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibrations of hydroxy and amino groups. The sharp peaks at 1620 (blue-CDs), 1665 (green-CDs) and 1661 cm−1 (yellow-CDs) are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO group of carboxylic and amide groups. The characteristic peaks at 1082 and 1130 cm−1 are assigned to the POC bending and SO3 hydrogen bonding vibrations. The typical peaks at 521, 926, 1086 and 1089 cm−1 are assigned to PO and PO stretching vibrations in green- and yellow-CDs Similarly, the zeta potentials (ζ) of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs are −31.14, −37.93 and −64.52 mV (Fig. S2 in Supporting Information), which signifies the presence of active organic functional groups (COOH, OH, NH2, CS and CP) on the surface of three CDs (Fig. S3 in Supporting Information). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs show peaks at 287, 288, and 286 eV, which belong to C 1 s of three CDs (Fig. S4 in Supporting Information). The peaks at 536 and 533 eV correspond to O 1s of three CDs and the peaks at ~287, ~399 and ~534 eV are assigned to the C1s, N1s and O1s of CDs, respectively. These results suggest that the existence of CC, CC, C=O/CN, and COH and CO groups in three CDs, respectively. These spectral characteristics are well agreed with the FT-IR spectra of three CDs. Thus, the as-synthesized three carbon dots contain active organic functional groups (hydrophilic) such as COOH, OH, NH2, CHO, and CO, respectively. The excitation dependent emission behaviors of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs were studied by measuring the emission spectra of CDs at different wavelengths in the range of 310–410, 380–480 and 390–500 nm (Fig. 1d–f). It is noted that the fluorescence emission spectra of three CDs exhibit the bathochromic emission characteristics and show the maximum emission peaks at 450, 520, and 560 nm under excitation wavelengths of 360, 420 and 460 nm for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs, respectively. These spectral characteristics are strongly correlated with the reported methods in the literature [46], confirming the fabricated CDs have different sizes with multiple emissive traps at their surfaces. The sizes and morphologies of three-color CDs were studied by using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (Fig. 2a–c). These results show that the as-fabricated blue-, green-, and yellow-CDs are in spherical shape in the ranging from ~5.0 to 10.0 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime is 11.86 ns for blue-CDs. These unique optical and spectral characteristics suggest that the as-fabricated three-color CDs can be used as fluorescent probes for sensing of chemical species and imaging of cells.

Fig. S5 in Supporting Information displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of as-prepared blue-, green- and yellow-CDs, respectively. The XRD spectrum of blue-CDs showed distinct sharp and slight broad diffraction peaks at 22.89, 23.30, 25.62, 32.04, and 34.20° whereas green-CDs showed characteristic sharp diffraction peaks at 22.70, 23.07, 23.79, 26.64, and 27.25°, respectively. Similarly, yellow-CDs displayed several characteristic sharp diffraction peaks at 21.97, 22.66, 26.34, 26.52, 27.17, and 28.04°, respectively. The diffraction data of three CDs revealed that the synthesized blue-, green- and yellow-CDs are in amorphous and crystalline natures with graphite lattice spacing, suggesting the existence of highly disordered carbon atoms [37,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of analytical performance of three fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the analytical performance of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs for sensing the metal ions, the responses of the emission spectra of three CDs toward various metal ions including Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ag+ and As5+ ions (200 μM) were investigated (Fig. 3a–c). Compared to other metal ions studied, only Fe3+ ion has shown significant quenching effect on fluorescence emission spectra of three CDs, revealing their selectivity to recognition of Fe3+ ion. Based on the above nature, blue-, green- and yellow-CDs were used as fluorescent sensors for selective and sensitive recognition of Fe3+ ion. As shown in Fig. 3d–e, the fluorescence intensities of three CDs were progressively quenched with increasing concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0.1 to 2.0 μM. As the quenching efficiencies (I0/I) exhibited good linear relationships (R2 = 0.9581, 0.8397 and 0.9520 for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs) versus Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.1–1.25 μM, where I0 and I represent the emission intensities of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs at 450, 520 and 560 nm without and with addition of Fe3+ ion (Fig. S6 in Supporting Information). The LODs are found to be 0.016, 0.072 and 0.065 μM for Fe3+ ion using blue-, green- and yellow-CDs, which were estimated according to 3 σ/m (σ is the standard deviation of the blank and “m” is the slope of the calibration graph). The LODs of the method can be compared with the reported methods [38,39] and exhibited lower than the reported methods in the literature [27,28,37,47–50] (Table S1 in Supporting Information). Importantly, the LODs of the method were lower than the permissible limits of Fe3+ ion (~5.357 μM) recommended by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [51].

To explore the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime decay of blue-CDs was investigated with and without addition of Fe3+ ion. The average fluorescence lifetime of blue-CDs is 11.86 ns. However, the lifetime of blue-CDs is reduced to 4.18 ns with the addition of Fe3+ ion, signifying the rapid electron-transfer mechanism between Fe3+ ion and three CDs, which confirms that the Fe3+ ion sensing is based on the dynamic quenching [48,52]. To support this mechanism, zeta potentials (ζ) of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs were measured with the addition of Fe3+ ion (Fig. S2b,d,f in Supporting Information). It was noticed that the ζ values of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs were changed to +16.18, −18.17 and −56.25 mV, suggesting the formation of Fe3+ ion-CDs complexes. As shown in Fig. S7 of Supporting Information, the FT-IR spectral characteristics of blue-, green-, and yellow-CDs were completely shifted or changed with the addition of Fe3+ ion, suggesting the formation of CDs-Fe3+ ion complexes.

Generally, the fluorescence “turn-off” (quenching) mechanism is mainly due to the strong interactions between fluorescent CDs and target analyte molecules, which leads either dynamic or static quenching. The as-synthesized CD surfaces consist of active organic functional groups (OH, COOH, CHO, NH2, PO, and PO) and exhibited strong emission peaks at 450, 520, and 560 nm for blue-, green- and yellow-CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. S8 in Supporting Information, the characteristic emission peaks intensities at 450, 520 and 560 nn were remarkably quenched upon the addition of Fe3+ ion, signifying the three CDs act as fluorescent probes for fluorescence “turn-off” detection of Fe3+ ion via quenching mechanism. The fluorescence quenching of three CDs emission peaks by Fe3+ ion confirms that the aggregation of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs induced by Fe3+ ion.

Further, HR-TEM images also confirm the aggregation of three CDs upon the addition of Fe3+ ion (Fig. 2c–d), signifying the strong interactions of Fe3+ ion with organic functional groups (OH, COOH, CHO, NH2, PO, and PO) of three CDs, which leads to form coordinated bonds. It was observed that among three CDs as fluorescent sensors, blue-CDs exhibited superior sensitivity than the green- and yellow-CDs for assaying of Fe3+ ions. Taken together, blue-, green- and yellow-CDs act as potential fluorescence “turn-off” sensors for assaying of Fe3+ ions.

The selectivity of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs toward Fe3+ ions over common inorganic species and pesticides were investigated (Fig. 4 ). It is worthy to note that the mixture of inorganic species (metal ions - Ba2+, Ca2+,Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe2+ and Al3+ ion, and anions - Cl−, F−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, S2−, PO4 3− and Cr2O7 2−, 100 μM) did not show any significant effect on quenching of fluorescence emission spectra of three CDs, whereas the addition of Fe3+ ion has remarkably quenched the fluorescence emission intensities of three CDs. These results indicate that three CDs exhibited high selectivity to detect Fe3+ ion in the presence of inorganic species.

Furthermore, the selectivity of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs toward Fe3+ ion sensing was evaluated in the presence of organic and biomolecules (urea, glucose, fructose, glutathione, bovine serum albumin, ascorbic acid, and amino acids (phenylalanine, threonine, valine, methionine, tryptophan, histidine, tyrosine, glutamine, and cysteine)), respectively. As exhibited in Fig. S8 of Supporting Information, there were no obvious emission peaks quenched by the addition of the above organic molecules. However, the emission peaks intensities of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs were remarkably quenched only after the addition of 1.0 μM of Fe3+ ion, demonstrating that the three CDs-based nanoprobes exhibited high selective fluorescence turn-off response toward Fe3+ ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of Fe3+ ion in biofluids and pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the potential applications of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs for assaying Fe3+ ions in biofluids (plasma and urine) and pharmaceutical samples were investigated. The recoveries of Fe3+ ion was estimated by spiking known concentrations of Fe3+ ion (2.50, 5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 μM) in biofluids. The Fe3+ ion concentration was determined by fabricated three fluorescent CDs. It is worthy to note that the recoveries were in the range of 95.80–97.90% and of 98.40–99.00% for plasma and urine samples, with relative standard deviation <1.2% (n = 3). We also investigated the practical applications of three fluorescent CDs for assaying Fe3+ ions in iron tablets. Briefly, iron supplements were purchased from a local medical shop (COFOL-Z, Cipla, India, 50 mg of iron), and 50 mg of tablet powder was treated with aqua regia (2.0 mL) to convert Fe2+ to Fe3+ ion. Following the sample dryness, the residue was transferred into a 100 mL standard flask and then make up with DI water. The concentration of Fe3+ ion was estimated by the present method. The amount of Fe3+ ion was found to be 49.33 ± 0.037 μg in the iron supplements (Table S2 in Supporting Information), revealing the potentiality of the method for assaying Fe3+ ions in real samples. These results indicated that the as-fabricated blue-, green- and yellow-CDs acted as promising fluorescent probes for assaying Fe3+ ions in biofluids and pharmaceutical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells using three fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the feasibility of three fluorescent CDs for cell imaging applications, HeLa cells were incubated with blue-, green- and yellow-CDs and studied their abilities by fluorescence microscopy. As shown in Fig. 5 , HeLa cells exhibited remarkable blue, green and yellow fluorescence emissions with blue-, green- and yellow-CDs under laser excitation wavelengths of 370 (blue), 420 (green) and 460 (yellow), respectively. It can be revealed that the as-fabricated three types of CDs are easily entered into cells and distributed in HeLa cell membrane and the cytoplasmic area, and exhibited blue, green and yellow fluorescence colors. MTT assays of blue-, green- and yellow-CDs against HeLa cells reveal that three fluorescent CDs showed promising biocompatible nature and exhibited non-toxic nature even at 1.0 mg/mL (Fig. S9 in Supporting Information). These results indicated that the as-fabricated blue-, green- and yellow-CDs acted as promising fluorescent probes for efficient Fe3+ ion sensing and HeLa cell imaging, which enhances the analytical performances of fluorescence techniques with lower LODs.

In summary, blue-, green- and yellow-fluorescent CDs were fabricated using tomato (S. lycopersicum) as a carbon source via chemical oxidation method. The as-fabricated blue, green and yellow fluorescent CDs exhibited emission peaks at 450, 520, and 560 nm under excitation wavelengths of 360, 420 and 460 nm. The analytical performances of three fluorescent CDs were evaluated for assaying Fe3+ ions in biofluids and pharmaceutical samples. Among three fluorescent CDs, blue-CDs exhibited superior analytical performance for the detection of Fe3+ ion than that of green and yellow CDs. Further, the analytical merit of the method is comparable with the reported methods and exhibited better sensitivity than that of reported methods for assaying Fe3+ ions. In addition, three fluorescent CDs exhibited good biocompatibility for imaging of HeLa cells and showed non-toxic behavior toward HeLa cells. Thus, the blue-, green- and yellow-CDs can be exploited as promising fluorescent probes for assaying Fe3+ ions in biofluids and pharmaceutical samples and for biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Brain Pool Program through the Korean Federation of Science and Technology Societies (KOFST) and Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (172S-4-3-1923; NRF-2017R1A2B4009581; NRF-2018R1A4A1022647). SKK acknowledges the Director, SVNIT for granting leave for Brain Pool Korea Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.01.002.
